hq: [Company Address] — address placeholder in report; not disclosed

system_description.overview: Morph (dba AutoInfra, Inc.) is an AI model provider for AI code generation companies, acting as a middleware layer between foundation models and engineering systems. Its flagship product Fast Apply enables safe, high-throughput deterministic code editing from LLM suggestions with ~98% accuracy and 4500+ token throughput. The platform also supports compliance automation across SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001, GDPR, and PCI DSS frameworks.

products.Fast Apply: Deterministic code editing — high-precision application of LLM-generated edits directly into source files with syntax-aware, structured incremental changes.
products.Contextual Intelligence Layer: High-recall embeddings for syntax-aware code search and retrieval; code-tuned reranker filters context for relevance to boost LLM accuracy.
products.Automated Compliance Management Platform: End-to-end AI-powered compliance automation supporting multiple frameworks with automated evidence collection, continuous compliance monitoring, and CI/CD integrations.
products.AI-Powered Security and Compliance Featu: Automated code scanning, automated infrastructure scanning, access control monitoring, and security questionnaire automation.
products.White-Glove Support Services: Expert onboarding, compliance implementation services, ongoing compliance support with real-time dashboards.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS SOC 2 report exists; AWS controls excluded from scope)
regions: Not explicitly specified — 'high availability data centers' referenced
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — report explicitly states no physical offices and no physical servers; all data processed on cloud services. CC6.4 and A1.2 excluded on this basis.
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — 'high availability data centers with multiple availability zones'; DR plan spins up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS
additional_cloud: Modal (LLM inference endpoints — primary and fallback)
serverless: AWS Lambda (API server — FastAPI on Modal or Lambda)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Hosted in AWS VPC with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). System components configured so company and customer access is appropriately segmented from other customer accounts (CC6.1.3).
network_architecture.api_gateway: Custom load balancer 'morph-api-proxy' with health monitoring, smart model routing, auto failover, and circuit breaker functionality
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings.
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN for remote access to production systems.
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority for all user requests to web-based systems.
network_architecture.content_filtering: Proxy server with content filtering system; all systems restricted to internet via proxy.
network_architecture.topology: Client App (API Client) and Dashboard (NextJS/Vercel) -> Load Balancer (morph-api-proxy: health monitoring, smart model routing, auto failover, circuit breaker) -> API Server (FastAPI on Modal or Lambda) + Clerk Auth -> Routing Decision Engine -> Modal Endpoints (primary/fallback/vLLM+Modal/serverless) or Self-Hosted Lambda Cloud (GH200 ARM, hardware-locked, encrypted models, vLLM+Docker, OpenAI API) -> LLM Inference Engine (vLLM Multi-node) -> PostgreSQL DB (neon); supporting services: Redis Cache (upstash), Authentication, Rate Limits, Stripe Integration, Metered Usage Logs.

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (microservices-oriented with serverless compute; routing decision engine directing between Modal cloud endpoints and self-hosted Lambda cloud)
compute: Self-hosted Lambda Cloud on GH200 ARM GPUs
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4); changes tested in dev/test before promotion to production.

file_storage: Not explicitly named — AWS S3 implied via AWS infrastructure
data_storage.backup_frequency: Formal backup policy exists; specific frequency not disclosed. DR plan references spinning up servers across all AZs in AWS.

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Clerk (Auth — shown explicitly in network diagram as 'Clerk Auth')
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3). Two-factor authentication required for employee email access.
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS password policy controls: periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements, account lockout after failed attempts; no guest or anonymous logins; inactivity lockout with password or biometric unlock required.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider KMS — 'organization uses its cloud provider key management service')

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named explicitly — 'source code management tool' with MFA-gated access; branch protection rules configured (implies GitHub or GitLab)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not named explicitly — changes tracked in 'internal tool' with ticketing/change-management application
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request moving changes to production environment (CC8.1.2).
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations with rollback capability (CC7.1.1) — specific tool not named
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: Docker (vLLM+Docker on self-hosted Lambda Cloud); no Kubernetes mentioned
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO/Management set up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations receive automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily; critical OS patches applied as available.

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — internal monitoring tools with predefined threshold alerting
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled at application and infrastructure level — specific tool not named (CloudWatch implied via AWS)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; resolved through incident management process.

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure — compute, networking, storage, KMS, IDS/IPS, VPC, Multi-AZ hosting
LLM inference serverless endpoints (primary, fallback, vLLM+Modal, serverless)
Payment processing / metered usage billing integration
AI API fallback (OpenAI API listed in self-hosted Lambda Cloud path)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection on all production servers with continuous scanning; AV definitions updated weekly on servers, daily on workstations. Installed on Mac-based workstations and laptops. Email scanning for malicious scripts and viruses.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications (tool not named); additional scan prior to every production release or yearly. High/critical vulnerabilities tracked to remediation via incident response plan.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; high/critical findings tracked to resolution.

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — physical access to data center facilities: AWS is responsible for physical security; Morph has no physical offices or servers
A1.2 — environmental protections, data backup processes, recovery infrastructure: Morph has no office/working space; all environmental controls managed by building management / AWS
Processing Integrity trust service category — determined not relevant to the system
Privacy trust service category — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.3 — Source code access restricted to authorized users using MFA; changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author
CC8.1.2 — Branch protection: every merge request to production requires additional approval; developers cannot directly change production code
CC6.1.1 — MFA enforced for all accounts with administrative access to production platform
CC6.1.4 — Access revocation within one business day of termination; termination checklist used
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
CC6.1.5 — Cloud provider KMS used for encryption keys at rest; production access keys restricted to authorized individuals
CC4.1 — Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
CC7.1.2 — Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications
CC7.4.1 — BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually; loss scenarios tested at least annually
Self-hosted LLM inference on GH200 ARM GPUs with hardware-locked, encrypted models — notable for an early-stage AI company

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ AWS deployment with VPN, IDS/IPS, VPC segmentation, and ACLs. Strong: explicit multi-AZ failover, carve-out AWS SOC 2, multiple specialized cloud providers for different functions (Modal, Neon, Upstash, Vercel). Deductions: single region only, no CDN/WAF explicitly named, no explicit IaC tool named.

application_architecture.rationale: Sophisticated hybrid architecture visible in detailed network diagram: FastAPI backend, Next.js frontend, routing decision engine with smart model routing and circuit breaker, dual inference paths (Modal cloud + self-hosted GH200 ARM). vLLM multi-node inference engine shows ML engineering maturity. Specific languages and frameworks named. Minor deduction: no explicit test coverage mention.

data_layer.rationale: Postgres (Neon) + Redis (Upstash) is a reasonable stack; per-tenant segregation confirmed; KMS at-rest encryption. Deductions: no data warehouse, backup frequency not specified, file storage not explicitly named, Neon is a third-party managed DB introducing additional vendor dependency.

security_posture.rationale: Strong: annual external pen test, monthly automated vuln scans, MFA on production/SCM, quarterly access reviews, IDS/IPS, firewalls, endpoint AV, TLS in transit, KMS at rest, log protection, 1-business-day access revocation. Gaps: no WAF explicitly named, no SIEM named, no named security tooling vendors, cybersecurity insurance not mentioned.

devops_maturity.rationale: Solid practices: branch protection requiring approvals, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch management, source code logged with MFA access, config management tool for IaC with rollback. Deductions: CI/CD tool and SCM platform not named, IaC tool not named, no containerization orchestration beyond Docker.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and annually reviewed, loss scenarios tested annually, multi-AZ failover capability with automatic server spin-up. Deductions: no RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, daily backup frequency not confirmed, DR detail sits in Section 5 (unaudited).

vendor_diversity.rationale: 8 distinct named vendors: AWS, Modal, Neon, Upstash, Vercel, Clerk, Stripe, OpenAI. Good functional separation. Concern: both primary cloud (AWS) and LLM inference (Modal) are single-vendor dependencies at their respective layers; loss of Modal would be critical. Vendor management policy with annual SOC reviews and quarterly assessments for non-SOC vendors is in place.

overall.rationale: Morph shows notable technical sophistication for an early-stage AI company (SOC 2 Type 1 with detailed architecture, explicit network diagram, hardware-locked GPU inference). Core security hygiene (MFA, quarterly access reviews, pen testing, vuln scanning, branch protection) is properly implemented. Main weaknesses: no named CI/CD or SIEM tools, no WAF, RTO/RPO undisclosed, single-region, and significant concentration in Modal for LLM inference.

red_flags: Modal is a critical single point of failure for LLM inference with no named fallback vendor SLA disclosed; Modal itself is a startup-stage infrastructure provider.
No WAF mentioned — the API is internet-facing and processes LLM inference requests at scale.
No SIEM or named log aggregation/alerting tool — monitoring described generically without vendor specificity.
RTO and RPO not disclosed — BCDR plan exists but recovery time commitments are unknown.
Org chart shows only 4 people total (CEO Tejas + 2 ML Engineers + 1 ML Intern) — extreme key-person dependency; entire engineering team is 2 people.
yellow_flags: Single AWS region — no multi-region failover; AZ failure coverage exists but regional outage would impact service.
Neon (serverless Postgres) is itself a startup-stage managed database vendor — adds vendor risk at the DB layer.
CI/CD tool and source control platform not named in the report — cannot assess maturity of pipeline tooling.
IaC tool referenced generically as 'configuration management tool' — Terraform/Pulumi/CloudFormation not confirmed.
Backup frequency not specified — formal policy exists but recovery point window is unknown.
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded — acceptable for this use case but worth noting for enterprise buyers.
HQ address listed as '[Company Address]' placeholder — suggests report was finalized with incomplete metadata.
Self-hosted inference on GH200 ARM hardware-locked GPUs introduces physical hardware dependency not fully covered by carve-out.
green_flags: Exceptionally detailed and transparent network diagram (page 19) showing full system topology including specific vendor names — rare for a Type 1 report.
Sophisticated routing architecture: morph-api-proxy with health monitoring, smart model routing, auto failover, and circuit breaker pattern — production-grade resilience design.
Dual inference paths (Modal cloud + self-hosted Lambda Cloud on GH200 ARM) provides inference-layer redundancy with model encryption at rest.
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed (CC4.1).
Monthly automated vulnerability scans with high/critical remediation tracked through incident management.
MFA enforced at production access, source code tool, and employee email — comprehensive MFA coverage.
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts (CC6.2.2).
1-business-day access revocation SLA with termination checklist (CC6.1.4).
Branch protection requiring additional approval on every merge to production (CC8.1.2).
Vendor management policy with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for non-SOC vendors (CC9.2.1).
BCDR tested annually across different loss scenarios — not just documented but exercised.
Platform targets compliance automation (SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001, GDPR, PCI DSS) — security is core to the product, creating alignment between internal practices and product offering.
key_observations: Morph is an AI infrastructure company auditing itself against SOC 2 — the product itself generates compliance automation and evidence, creating an interesting alignment: Morph likely uses its own tooling for its compliance program.

The network diagram reveals Modal as the primary LLM inference provider, which is itself a venture-backed GPU cloud startup (not a hyperscaler). This is an unusual and notable vendor concentration risk for an AI API company.

Self-hosted GH200 ARM inference cluster with 'hardware-locked, encrypted models' suggests Morph may be running proprietary fine-tuned models they want to protect from extraction — not just routing to commodity APIs.

The org chart (page 21) shows a 4-person company at audit time: CEO (Tejas Bhakta) plus 2 ML Engineers (Dat, Suraj) and 1 ML Intern (Dhruv). This is an extremely early-stage company obtaining SOC 2 Type 1 proactively.

Clerk is used for authentication rather than AWS Cognito or a traditional enterprise IdP (Okta/Auth0) — Clerk is developer-focused and relatively new; worth noting for enterprise buyers requiring enterprise SSO.

The report's geographic scope table explicitly states 'No physical offices' — Morph is fully remote with no physical infrastructure, which simplifies the physical security scope but means all resilience depends on AWS and third-party SaaS vendors.
Stripe integration for metered usage billing is visible in the architecture diagram, confirming usage-based pricing model for the API product.
The report was signed on 8/7/2025 with a report date of July 30, 2025 — very recent audit, suggesting this is Morph's first SOC 2.
